title: Apple, pear sector stakeholders meet for annual conference
description_clean: New Zealand apple and pear industry stakeholders are out in force for the annual conference in Queenstown. Growers, exporters, researchers, government representatives and industry partners alike have gathered to discuss the challenges and opportunities that are shaping the sector’s future. The first day of the two-day conference yesterday featured a packed programme of speakers and panel discussions covering topics including research and development, innovation, biosecurity, and what’s likely in the future for New Zealand’s apple and pear growers. NZAPI’s annual general meeting was also held o
summary: New Zealand apple and pear industry stakeholders gathered in Queenstown for a two-day annual conference featuring speakers and panel discussions on research, innovation, biosecurity, and the sector's future. NZAPI also held its annual general meeting.

title: The Banker Who Still Farms: Cameron Reed's Two Worlds
description_clean: Cameron Reed lives between two worlds. One day he might be in a boardroom, making calls on the agri strategy for New Zealand’s largest bank across the lower half of the South Island. The next, he’s back on the family farm in Darfield, putting up a break fence while reflecting on the same pressures his customers are facing. Reed is ANZ’s provincial southern head of business and agri, overseeing the bank’s business and farm lending across the lower South Island, while also carrying responsibility for helping to shape ANZ’s agri strategy and direction. His family has been farming in Darfield for
summary: Cameron Reed, ANZ's provincial southern head of business and agri, balances overseeing the bank's farm lending across the lower South Island with farming on his family's Darfield property.

title: Global Shocks Are No Longer Distant
description_clean: OPINION: At Fieldays this year, one theme kept surfacing across the sector: global shocks are no longer something that happens “over there”. They are now translating quickly into local, farm-level impacts through increasingly complex and connected channels. New Zealand’s agribusiness sector has long been defined by its ability to adapt. Droughts, floods, biosecurity events and global market cycles have made resilience a core part of how the sector operates. That resilience is not accidental. It is the product of decades of structural change and a culture of adaptation. What has emerged is a se
summary: An opinion piece from Fieldays argues global shocks now translate quickly into local farm-level impacts, and highlights New Zealand's agribusiness resilience built through decades of structural change and adaptation.

title: Farm Safety Improves Through 'Incremental Gain', Says Birkett
description_clean: Improving health and safety is about incremental gain rather than a sudden decision, says Leeston farmer and Federated Farmers health and safety spokesman David Birkett. The former arable section chair recently hosted an event on his farm, which was organised by the Safer Farms advocacy group and celebrated a big reduction in ACC claims from on-farm accidents over the last five years. Claims are down from about 25,000 in 2020 to 16,666 last year. Offering some practical tips on improving farm safety, Birkett told the gathering that farmers should look at health and safety angles whenever faced
summary: Federated Farmers health and safety spokesman David Birkett says farm safety improves through incremental gains, noting on-farm ACC claims fell from about 25,000 in 2020 to 16,666 last year.

title: Trust Can Only Be Earned
description_clean: OPINION: Yep, the stories just keep on coming. I haven’t noted any sign of them drying up yet, no let-up seems to be in sight! And with the rapid advances in AI, maybe the worst is still to come. November 17, 2025, the Ministry of Business, Innovation and Employment informed us that scammers had defrauded New Zealanders out of $265 million over the last 12 months. That’s right. And the payments for $126 million of that total was authorised by the very account holders themselves. The suggestion was made that the actual total was probably even higher than the $126m because many would have been t
summary: An opinion piece discusses trust and scams, noting MBIE reported scammers defrauded New Zealanders of $265 million over 12 months, with $126 million authorised by account holders themselves.

title: True Reflection?
description_clean: A fair question has been raised about how member-funded organisations should conduct themselves when taking a public position on any given issue. The commonsense view, this mutt reckons, is that the organisation, its board and executive branch ultimately exist to serve its membership and represent their views. Yet, time and again, these organisations seem to act unilaterally without consulting the guys and gals that pay the directors’ fees and management salaries. The latest example raising hackles was Rural Women NZ lodging a submission opposing the Definition of a Man and Woman Bill. There’s
summary: An opinion piece questions whether member-funded organisations adequately consult members before taking public positions, citing Rural Women NZ's submission opposing the Definition of a Man and Woman Bill.

title: Editorial: The Price of Progress
description_clean: OPINION: Alistair Carmichael raises some interesting questions about striking a balance between profitability in farming and preserving the character of rural communities. NZ is lauded by many other nations for maximising profitability and efficiency in the primary sector and this philosophy has served us well. We don’t subsidise our farmers like many countries in the Northern Hemisphere and in other parts of the world and that is reflected in the export dollar the primary sector brings to the benefit of all Kiwis. This mantra works well for us and is a good selling point. At the same time, ou
summary: An editorial discusses balancing farming profitability with preserving rural community character, noting New Zealand's unsubsidised primary sector model and its export contribution.

title: Stuffed!
description_clean: The canine crusader always has his nose in the air for more media maleficence, so was pointing as soon as he saw the Stuff headline: ‘Wellington residents respond to PM saying he doesn’t need his job!’. A case of intrepid reporters recording the news as neutral bystanders? Yeah, right! Yet another case of woke media luvvies trying to screw the scrum in election year and acting as the unofficial morality police. The story kicked off with a little lecture, scolding the PM Chris Luxon for being rich and bragging about it, while “hundreds of [public service] workers could lose their jobs”. Then th
summary: An opinion piece criticises a Stuff article about PM Chris Luxon's comments on his job, accusing the media of bias amid public service job losses in an election year.

title: NZ Ploughing Champions' Gear Stuck at Sea Before Worlds
description_clean: Ploughing champions Mark Dillon and Malcolm Taylor earned the right to represent the country at the World Ploughing Championships in Osijek, Croatia, on 5 and 6 September, after winning their titles at the New Zealand Ploughing Championships in Middlemarch in 2025. With ploughing being a dark art, the duo has spent many years tweaking their tractors, ploughs and accessories to help gain those extra few points and achieve a win. To give themselves the best chance of success, a plan made, shipping in April, contracting freight specialists Oceanbridge, who would use MSC Shipping to move the team'
summary: New Zealand ploughing champions Mark Dillon and Malcolm Taylor, set to compete at the World Ploughing Championships in Croatia in September, have had their gear stuck at sea despite arranging shipping in April.

title: Gallagher Adds Second Production Line as Wearables Boom
description_clean: The man appointed to lead Gallagher's global Animal Management business through the next stage of growth believes the wearables boom is here. Rob Clayton says a second production line for its eShepherd virtual fencing is now up and running at its Hamilton site, as the company strives to meet growing global demand for remote farm management technology. A third production line is also being proposed. "The boom's already happening and we've just built another production line," Clayton told Rural News . "We could not keep up with demand; we couldn't build them as fast as people want to buy them."
summary: Gallagher has added a second production line for its eShepherd virtual fencing at its Hamilton site to meet growing global demand, with a third line proposed, as leader Rob Clayton says the wearables boom is underway.

title: Moxham Family's Organic Milk Now Comes as Gelato
description_clean: Horowhenua organic dairy farmers Ange and Wayne Moxham have taken their business to a new level. They have just been named as Fonterra's top organic performer for milksolids and that coincides with them developing a new exciting product from their milk - namely gelato to complement their organic milk sales. The Moxhams say they are delighted and greatly encouraged to receive the award and say it's also a testament to their staff on their two farms in Koputaroa, a small rural community about 8km north of Levin. The larger of the two farms runs 500 cows on 240 hectares, and the smaller 70ha one
summary: Horowhenua organic dairy farmers Ange and Wayne Moxham, named Fonterra's top organic performer for milksolids, have developed a gelato product to complement their organic milk sales from their two Koputaroa farms.
